summary:
The suture pattern most commonly employed for repairing an episiotomy—especially one requiring tissue approximation under tension—is likely a vertical mattress stitch because it provides effective support by reducing strain across the repaired area while maintaining structural integrity during healing. This choice reflects established guidelines where stable closure after childbirth trauma necessitates strong fixation without excessive stretching at either end of the incision site.

Final Answer: D
The answer provided corresponds correctly to option D — Vertical Mattress Sutures being appropriate given typical obstetric practice patterns related to perineal laceration management.

Okay, I see you're asking me to summarize only Agent2’s reasoning about what kind of suturing technique was used based solely on clinical relevance from the text content shared earlier.

Let me now extract just two concise sentences focusing strictly on clinically significant elements:

Vertical mattress sutures are preferred for episiotomy repair because they effectively reduce tensile stress across the surgical site and maintain tissue alignment throughout recovery.  
This approach ensures reliable wound stabilization post-delivery when closing traumatic tears in the perineum—a common scenario demanding durable yet flexible closure methods.
